subtopics.title: Introduction to Grammar – Parts of Speech & Core Concepts

blocks.title: What is Grammar?
blocks.content: Grammar is the system of rules that governs the structure of language. It includes syntax (word order), morphology (word forms), and semantics (meaning).

rows.right: Names a person, place, thing, or idea (e.g., John, London, table).
Replaces a noun (e.g., he, she, it, they).
Shows action or state of being (e.g., run, is, have).
Modifies a noun or pronoun (e.g., beautiful, tall).
Modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb (e.g., quickly, very).
Shows relationship between a noun/pronoun and another word (e.g., on, in, at).
Connects words, phrases, or clauses (e.g., and, but, because).

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
points: Understanding parts of speech is essential for mastering grammar.
Every word in a sentence belongs to at least one part of speech.
Words can change function depending on context (e.g., 'run' as a verb vs noun).

subtopics.title: Present Tenses – Indefinite, Continuous, Perfect & Perfect Continuous

blocks.title: Present Tenses
blocks.subtitle: Actions Happening Now, Regularly, or with Present Relevance

blocks.title: Present Tense Structures
blocks.formula:
1. Present Indefinite: Subject + V1 (s/es)
2. Present Continuous: Subject + is/am/are + V1+ing
3. Present Perfect: Subject + has/have + V3
4. Present Perfect Continuous: Subject + has/have + been + V1+ing
blocks.description: Time expressions: always, often, never, every day (Indefinite); now, at the moment (Continuous); already, yet, just, ever (Perfect); since, for (Perfect Continuous).

blocks.title: Example 1: Present Indefinite (Habit)
given: She (go) to the gym every morning.
steps: Present Indefinite is used for habits/routines.
Subject is 'She' (third person singular) → add 'es' to go → 'goes'.
blocks.answer: She goes to the gym every morning.

blocks.title: Example 2: Present Perfect (Completed action with present relevance)
steps: Present Perfect is used for past actions with present relevance.
Keyword: 'already' signals Present Perfect.
I + have + V3 (finished).
blocks.answer: I have finished my homework already.

blocks.title: Present Tenses – Quick Reference
rows: The sun rises in the east.
She has been working for 2 hours.

blocks.title: Trap: Using Simple Present for Present Continuous situations.
blocks.tip: Stative verbs (know, like, belong, own, etc.) are NOT used in continuous forms.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Use 's/es' with third person singular in Indefinite.
Use 'is/am/are + V1+ing' for ongoing actions.
Use 'has/have + V3' for finished actions with present impact.
Use 'has/have been + V1+ing' for duration.
Stative verbs do not take continuous form.

subtopics.title: Past Tenses – Indefinite, Continuous, Perfect & Perfect Continuous

blocks.title: Past Tenses
blocks.subtitle: Actions Completed or Ongoing in the Past

blocks.title: Past Tense Structures
blocks.formula:
1. Past Indefinite: Subject + V2
2. Past Continuous: Subject + was/were + V1+ing
3. Past Perfect: Subject + had + V3
4. Past Perfect Continuous: Subject + had been + V1+ing
blocks.description: Time expressions: yesterday, last week (Indefinite); while, when (Continuous); before, after, by the time (Perfect); since, for (Perfect Continuous).

blocks.title: Example 1: Past Perfect (Earlier past action)
given: The train (leave) before I reached the station.
steps: Two past actions: 'reached' is simple past. The train left before that.
Earlier action needs Past Perfect: had + V3 (left).
blocks.answer: The train had left before I reached the station.

blocks.title: Example 2: Past Continuous (Interrupted action)
given: I (watch) TV when the power went off.
steps: Action was in progress when another action interrupted it.
Use Past Continuous: was/were + V1+ing.
I + was + watching.
blocks.answer: I was watching TV when the power went off.

blocks.title: Past Tenses – Quick Reference
rows: She had left before I called.
They had been waiting for an hour.

blocks.title: Trap: Using Past Perfect unnecessarily.
blocks.tip: If the sequence of past actions is clear (using 'then', 'after'), past indefinite is preferred.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: V2 is used for past indefinite (regular verbs end in -ed).
Use Past Perfect for the earlier of two past actions.
Use Past Continuous for ongoing past actions, especially interrupted ones.

subtopics.title: Future Tenses – Indefinite, Continuous, Perfect & Perfect Continuous

blocks.title: Future Tense Structures
blocks.formula:
1. Future Indefinite: Subject + will/shall + V1
2. Future Continuous: Subject + will be + V1+ing
3. Future Perfect: Subject + will have + V3
4. Future Perfect Continuous: Subject + will have been + V1+ing
blocks.description: Time expressions: tomorrow, next week, in two days, by 2025, by the time.

blocks.title: Example 1: Future Perfect (Completion by a certain time)
steps: By 2025 signals a deadline in the future.
Use Future Perfect: will have + V3.
blocks.answer: By 2025, I will have completed my degree.

blocks.title: Example 2: Future Continuous (Action in progress in future)
given: This time tomorrow, I (fly) to London.
steps: Action will be in progress at a specific future time.
Use Future Continuous: will be + V1+ing.
blocks.answer: This time tomorrow, I will be flying to London.

blocks.title: Future Tenses – Quick Reference
rows: Actions in progress at a future time
She will be working at 9 am.
He will have finished by 5 pm.
Duration up to a future time
I will have been working for 5 years.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: 'Will' is used for most future situations.
'Shall' is used with I/we in formal British English.
Use Future Perfect for deadlines in the future.
Use Future Continuous for ongoing future actions.

subtopics.title: Modal Verbs – Can, Could, May, Might, Must, Should, Would, etc.

blocks.title: Modal Verbs
blocks.subtitle: Expressing Ability, Permission, Obligation, and Possibility

blocks.title: What are Modals?
blocks.content: Modals are auxiliary verbs that express necessity, possibility, permission, or ability. They are used with the base form of the main verb (V1).

blocks.title: Modals – Meanings and Uses
rows: I can swim. Can I go? It can be difficult.
I could run fast. Could you help me? It could rain.
May I come in? It may rain today.
You must stop. It must be true.
You should study. It should work.
Polite request, habitual past, unreal situations
Would you like tea? He would come every day. I would do it if I could.
I shall return. Shall we dance?

blocks.title: Example 1: Modal of Obligation
given: You (must/should) not smoke in public places.
steps: Smoking in public is prohibited by law.
'Must not' indicates prohibition or strong obligation.
'Should not' is advice, not a legal rule.
blocks.answer: You must not smoke in public places.

blocks.title: Example 2: Modal of Past Ability
given: When I was young, I (can/could) climb any tree.
steps: Past ability requires 'could'.
'Can' is used for present ability.
blocks.answer: When I was young, I could climb any tree.

blocks.title: Trap: Using 'may' for certainty.
blocks.correct: It will definitely rain today. / It may rain today.
blocks.tip: 'May' shows possibility (less than 100%). Use 'will' for certainty.

blocks.title: Advanced: Modal Perfects
blocks.content:
Modal + have + V3: Must have (logical conclusion), Should have (past advice not taken), Could have (past ability not used), May have (past possibility), Might have (past less certain possibility).
Example: 'He must have left already' (I'm sure he left).

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Modals do not change form (no -s, -ed, -ing).
They are always followed by V1 (base form).
'Must not' is prohibition; 'don't have to' is absence of obligation.
Modal perfects are used for past situations.

blocks.title: What are Articles?
blocks.content: Articles are words that define a noun as specific or unspecific. 'A/An' are indefinite, 'The' is definite.

blocks.title: Article Rules
blocks.formula:
1. A – used before words starting with a consonant sound (a book, a university).
2. An – used before words starting with a vowel sound (an apple, an hour).
3. The – used for specific/unique items (the sun, the book on the table).
blocks.description: Remember: The choice of 'a/an' depends on the SOUND, not just the first letter.

blocks.title: Example 1: Choosing A vs An
steps: University starts with 'u' which makes a consonant sound (yoo).
So, use 'a'.

blocks.title: Example 2: Choosing The vs No Article
steps: Abstract nouns (honesty, truth, beauty) generally do not take an article.
However, 'the best' is a superlative, and 'the' is used with superlatives.
blocks.formula_used: No article before abstract nouns; 'the' before superlatives.

blocks.title: Article Usage – Quick Reference
rows: a cat, a university, a one-eyed man
an apple, an hour, an honest man
the Taj Mahal, the book I bought
Dogs are friendly. Honesty is good.

blocks.title: Trap: Using 'an' with 'university'.
blocks.tip: Focus on the sound: university sounds like 'yoo-ni-ver-si-ty' – consonant sound.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: A/An are indefinite – used for first mention.
The is definite – used for specific or unique things.
No article is used with plural countable nouns and most uncountable nouns when speaking generally.
Remember the SOUND rule for a/an.

subtopics.title: Prepositions – Time, Place, Movement, and Fixed Expressions

blocks.title: What are Prepositions?
blocks.content: Prepositions are words that show the relationship between a noun/pronoun and another word in the sentence. Common ones: in, on, at, by, with, for, from, under, over, etc.

blocks.title: Prepositions of Time, Place, and Movement
rows: at the door, at the table
on the table, on the bus
by the river, by the door
I have been here since morning.

blocks.title: Example 1: Preposition of Time
given: We have a meeting ___ Monday morning.
steps: For specific days, use 'on'.
'In' is used for months/years, 'at' for specific times.
blocks.answer: We have a meeting on Monday morning.

blocks.title: Example 2: Preposition of Place
steps: If you are sitting on a surface (chair), use 'on'.
'In' is used for vehicles (in a car) or enclosed spaces.
blocks.answer: She is sitting on the chair.

blocks.title: Trap: Confusing 'In' and 'Into'.
blocks.tip: 'In' indicates position; 'Into' indicates movement toward the inside.

blocks.title: Advanced: Fixed Prepositional Phrases
blocks.content:
Many verbs and adjectives take fixed prepositions: 
- Interested in, Good at, Afraid of, Depend on, Succeed in, etc.
Memorize common collocations (e.g., responsible for, proud of, satisfied with).

blocks.title: Key Points
points: At = specific time/point. In = larger periods (months/years). On = days/dates.
In = enclosed space. On = surface. At = point/position.
Into = movement (inside), In = position (inside).
Learn fixed prepositional phrases (verb + prep).

subtopics.title: Advanced Grammar – Conditionals, Subjunctive, Modal Perfects

blocks.title: Conditional Sentences
blocks.content: Conditional sentences express 'if' scenarios. There are 4 types: Zero (always true), First (likely future), Second (unreal present/future), Third (unreal past).

blocks.title: Conditional Types – Structure & Usage
rows: If you heat ice, it melts.
If it rains, I will stay home.
If I were rich, I would travel.
If I had known, I would have come.

blocks.title: Example 1: Third Conditional (Unreal Past)
given: She (pass) the exam if she (study) harder.
steps: This is an unreal past situation (she didn't study).
If clause: Past Perfect (had studied).
Main clause: would have + V3 (would have passed).
blocks.answer: She would have passed the exam if she had studied harder.
blocks.formula_used: If + had + V3, would have + V3

blocks.title: Example 2: Subjunctive Mood
steps: After suggest, recommend, insist, etc., use base form (V1) in the that-clause.
This is the subjunctive mood.
blocks.answer: I suggest that he study more.

blocks.title: Modal Perfects Recap
blocks.content: Must have = strong deduction. Should have = past advice (missed opportunity). Could have = ability not used. May have = possibility. Might have = less certain possibility.

blocks.title: Trap: Using 'was' instead of 'were' in Second Conditional.
blocks.tip: Always use 'were' (subjunctive) in the second conditional, regardless of the subject.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Zero: If + Present → Present.
First: If + Present → will + V1.
Second: If + Past → would + V1.
Third: If + Past Perfect → would have + V3.
Subjunctive: Base form after suggest, insist, etc.
Modal Perfects help express past deductions, advice, and possibilities.

subtopics.title: Common Grammar Errors – Subject-Verb Agreement, Tenses, Prepositions

blocks.title: Trap 1: Subject-Verb Agreement with Collective Nouns
blocks.tip: Collective nouns (team, group, committee) take singular verbs when acting as a unit.

blocks.title: Trap 2: 'Each' and 'Every' are Singular
blocks.wrong: Each of the boys are responsible.
blocks.correct: Each of the boys is responsible.

blocks.title: Trap 3: 'Since' vs 'For'
blocks.wrong: I have been here since two hours.
blocks.correct: I have been here for two hours.
blocks.tip: 'Since' is used with a point in time (since 2020). 'For' is used with a duration (for 2 hours).

blocks.title: Trap 4: 'Less' vs 'Fewer'
blocks.wrong: I have less apples than you.
blocks.correct: I have fewer apples than you.
blocks.tip: 'Fewer' is used with countable nouns (apples). 'Less' is used with uncountable nouns (water).

blocks.title: Trap 5: 'Who' vs 'Whom'
blocks.tip: 'Who' is the subject; 'Whom' is the object (Whom did you see? – you saw him).

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Collective nouns take singular verbs.
Each/Every/Either/Neither take singular verbs.
Since = point in time, For = duration.
Fewer = countable, Less = uncountable.
Who = subject, Whom = object.

blocks.title: Exam Strategy
blocks.content: Read the entire sentence. Check the subject-verb agreement. Look for time expressions to identify the correct tense. Verify prepositions and articles.

blocks.question: He ______ to the office every day.
blocks.explanation: 'Every day' indicates a routine. Subject is 'He' (third person singular) → 'goes'.

blocks.question: She ______ already ______ her homework.
blocks.explanation: 'Already' signals Present Perfect. Subject 'She' → 'has'. V3 of finish is 'finished'.

blocks.question: You ______ stop at the red signal.
blocks.explanation: Red signal indicates a legal obligation → 'must'.

blocks.question: She is ______ honest woman.
blocks.explanation: 'Honest' starts with a vowel sound (o). So, 'an' is used.

blocks.question: We have been working ______ 10 am.
blocks.explanation: 10 am is a specific point in time → use 'since'.

blocks.question: If I ______ you, I would accept the offer.
blocks.explanation: Second conditional (unreal present) uses 'were' for all subjects.

blocks.question: The furniture ______ very expensive.
blocks.explanation: 'Furniture' is an uncountable noun, hence singular verb 'is'.

blocks.question: He has ______ money than I have.
By 2026, they ______ construction of the building.
blocks.explanation: 'By 2026' signals a deadline in the future → Future Perfect (will have completed).

blocks.title: Post-Test Review
blocks.content: Review subject-verb agreement rules, the usage of 'since' vs 'for', and the sound-based rule for 'a/an'. Practice conditional and subjunctive structures.

blocks.title: Final Revision – Grammar
points: Tenses: Identify time expressions to choose the correct tense.
Modals: Must (obligation), Can (ability), Could (past ability), Should (advice).
Articles: A/An = sound-based; The = specific.
Prepositions: At (time/place), In (longer time/enclosed space), On (day/surface).
Conditionals: If + Present → will; If + Past → would; If + Past Perfect → would have.
Subjunctive: Base form after suggest/insist.
Common errors: Collective nouns (singular), Each/Every (singular), Since/For, Less/Fewer.
